name: Tarte Tatin — caramelised apple tart
description:
### About this dish
Tarte Tatin is a French upside-down tart with caramelised apples and buttery pastry. The pastry sits on top while baking, and the whole thing is inverted onto the plate when it comes out of the oven, exposing the lacquered apples that have soaked up the caramel.

### History
Invented by accident in the 1880s by the Tatin sisters at their hotel-restaurant in Lamotte-Beuvron, in the Loire Valley. The story goes that Stéphanie left the apples cooking in butter and sugar too long, then dropped a pastry round on top and turned the whole thing over after baking. The accidental order — apples first, dough on top — is what makes the dish.

### Ingredients
- 6 firm apples (Reinette des Canada or Cox), peeled, halved, cored
- 100 g unsalted butter
- 150 g caster sugar
- 1 disc of shortcrust or puff pastry, around 24 cm

### Method
Melt butter and sugar in a heavy ovenproof skillet until amber. Pack apple halves cut-side up. Cook on the stovetop for 20 minutes, turning once, until the caramel is deep amber and the apples soft. Lay the pastry over, tuck edges in around the apples. Bake at 200 °C for 25 minutes until the pastry is golden. Cool 10 minutes, then invert onto a plate.

### Serve
Warm, with crème fraîche or vanilla ice cream.
